verdict_reasoning: HSPD1 encodes HSP60, a chaperone protein resident in the mitochondrial matrix. Its primary localization is intramitochondrial; it functions as a 60 kDa heat-shock protein facilitating protein folding within the mitochondrial compartment. While HSP60 can be released during cell stress or damage and may transiently appear in the cytoplasm or extracellular space as a damage-associated molecular pattern (DAMP), there is no evidence of a constitutive or stable mechanism by which the protein integrates into or anchors to the plasma membrane outer leaflet. Release to the extracellular space is a consequence of mitochondrial rupture or cell lysis, not an independent mechanism for surface accessibility. The protein lacks transmembrane domains, GPI anchors, or documented stable partnership with surface-anchored proteins. Extracellular HSP60 functions as a secreted/released soluble protein and does not meet the criterion of stable outer-leaflet presence via its own mechanism.
